data.title: Nidec: Challenged By Margins, But Executing On Growth Opportunities
data.description:
I’ve liked Nidec (OTCPK:NJDCY) (OTCPK:NNDNF) (6594.T) for quite some time for the company’s strong knowhow in motors and the opportunity to leverage that knowhow into attractive markets like electric vehicles, robotics, and data centers. While that thesis has worked out (more or less) as far as product development and revenue goes, margin leverage has been underwhelming and a recent unsolicited bid for a rival machine tool company has me questioning the company’s strategic priorities.

Nidec shares are down about 20% since my last update, doing a little better than some rivals like Denso (OTCPK:DNZOF), DMG Mori (OTCPK:MRSKF) and Minebea Mitsumi (OTCPK:MNBEF), but lagging others like Sanyo Denki (6516.T), as well as proxies for key markets like EVs and appliances like Valeo (OTCPK:VLEEY) and Whirlpool (WHR).

Mid-single-digit free cash flow growth, EBITDA margins in the mid-teens, and a return to high single-digit ROICs can support a fair value 25% to 40% above today’s price. Management credibility is very much in question, though, and this is a company that badly needs to rebuild investor trust concerning margin leverage, capital allocation, and long-term strategic priorities.

Making A Bid For Makino – Why?

Arguably the biggest development since my last update is the company’s late December announcement of an unsolicited bid for Makino Milling (OTCPK:MKMLF) (6135.T), the third-largest machine tool company in Japan (and, I believe, the third-largest in the world).

Nidec is looking to buy Makino for 11,000 JPY/share, or a 42% premium to the share price before the announcement (and around 11.4x trailing EBITDA). Acquiring Makino would vault the combined companies to #2 in the machine tool market (behind DMG Mori) and would blend Makino’s more machining center-focused portfolio with Nidec’s more 5-axis-focused portfolio, as well as combine complementary end-market strengths (Makino is stronger in areas like healthcare, aerospace, and autos, Nidec in more general industrial, rail, and robotics).

I find this bid at least somewhat curious and concerning.

For starters, Makino management is clearly not enthusiastic about the bid, and Makino’s current share price already anticipates that Nidec will have to up its offer. Second, while I can definitely see operational and financial synergies in this deal – broadening Nidec’s machine tool offerings and customer exposure, as well as improving the arguably underwhelming financial performance of Makino (single-digit ROEs) – the reality is that the global machine tool market is a deeply cyclical industry with not all that much “peak to peak” growth now, and lower-cost Chinese and South Korean companies have been gaining share.

I can appreciate an opportunistic bid for an underperforming company heading toward a cyclical recovery. Likewise, I can appreciate the synergy benefits of the deal. But I just don’t love a $1.6B bid (around 7% of Nidec’s EV) when that money could go toward more interesting opportunities like robotics and automation – areas where Nidec is already active. Of course, given the scale of the deal, acquiring Makino doesn’t necessarily preclude other deal, but I worry about management diluting their focus at a time when execution is still very much an issue in investors’ minds.

Revenue Is Coming In Nicely, But Margin Leverage Is Lacking

Since my last update on Nidec, the company has reported earnings for three quarters, and revenue has been tracking ahead of my expectations (and I was relatively bullish with my revenue expectations).

The Small Precision Motors business logged 18% year-over-year growth in the third quarter, with 13% and 25% growth in the prior two quarters, and the company is seeing strong near-line hard disk drive shipments and other precision motor sales (like fans for electronics/IT products). Auto sales have been decelerating, but were still up 9% in the last quarter, despite slowdowns in the global BEV market and weakness in the overall European auto market.

Other segments like Appliances / Commercial / Industrial and Machinery are also growing well (up 9% and 13%, respectively), with management benefitting from strong demand for newer offerings like backup power generators and battery storage systems, as well as some evidence of cyclical recovery in industrial machinery.

Margin performance has been less impressive. Gross margin declined about two points in fiscal Q3 (to 20.2%), and operating income was up just 5% on 11% revenue growth (with margin down 40bp yoy to 8.4%), with auto margins still in the low single-digits and both Precision Motors and ACI seeing year-over-year erosion.

This is not a new issue. Where Nidec once reliably generated EBITDA margins of 16% or better, it's been several years since that's been the case (FY'21 was close, but FY'17 was the last year of 16%-plus EBITDA margin).

Increased competition in once-dominant areas like hard drive spindle motors certainly hasn't helped, and the company has seen its gross margin fall more than two points over the last decade, partly due to a lack of scale in newer businesses. R&D spending has been pretty steady, but SG&A spending has grown from around 8% of of revenue to closer to 11%.

I attribute at least some of the higher SG&A to multiple M&A transactions over the last decade (including 8 since 2020) and management's decision to grant a lot of autonomy to those businesses. The company has also been investing fairly aggressively to lay the groundwork for growth in geographies like China and markets like EV traction motors.

Along the way there have been multiple restructuring efforts, including the implementation of IoT to boost plant productivity, copying best practices across units, and steps to enhance manufacturing efficiencies. But with meaningful charges for restructuring in multiple years since FY'18, including a "drastic reform" effort begun in early 2023 (called "WPR-X"), there still hasn't been a lot of concrete progress and sell-side expectations have continued to come down for the near term. I think is weighing more significantly on the share price (there is a strong, but underappreciated, link between industrial company margins and valuation multiples), but I do think there are drivers that can turn this around over time, including increased scale in the traction motor business and consolidation of some units with overlapping end-markets.

There Are Still Positives

For all of my frustrations that Nidec shares haven’t performed as I’d hoped, there are still positives that merit repeating.

The company continues to do a strong job of expanding into complementary markets – in a relatively short period of time, its liquid cooling products for data centers have grown to around 1% of revenue (and that’s with really only Super Micro (SMCI) as a major customer). Likewise, the company has built upon its expertise in motor and power systems to develop very competitive backup power generators for the data center market, and battery storage systems for a host of markets/businesses that want to embrace renewable energy/electrification.

The company likewise remains a significant player in emerging EV opportunities, with Nidec enjoying good supply relationships with the likes of Geely (GELLY), Peugeot, and Stellantis (STLA). As I expect more auto OEMs to move away from in-house development of EV powertrains (with investors pushing back on the cost of these projects), Nidec stands to be a winner long term, even if the near-term margin pressures have been worse than expected.

Last and not least, Nidec remains leveraged to a lot of attractive growth opportunities. Near-line hard drives still very much have a place in data centers, and while Nidec has lost its once-dominant market share in spindle motors to a more competitive Minebea, they’re still the market leader and the market is still growing. Likewise, while the growth of the EV market has been slower than initially hoped, it is still growing. Longer term, given that motors consume around half of the world’s electrical output, the demand for higher-performance, more efficient motors is not going to diminish, particularly with “automate and electrify everything” still in place as a long-term trend.

The Outlook

Given longer timelines for EV adoption and increased global macroeconomic risk (the impact of tariffs on industrial machinery, consumer appliances, and so on), I’ve reduced some of my growth expectations for the next three to five years despite the fact that fiscal 2025 has tracked better than I’d expected. I’m now looking for three-to-five year revenue growth of around 7%, with longer-term growth in the 6% to 7% range (versus a long-term trailing growth rate of around 6%).

Margin leverage is clearly a key debate and there’s not as much visibility here as I’d like. I do still expect full-year EBITDA margin in the 14%’s (up from less than 13% last year and less than 10% the year prior), but I’m less ambitious about Nidec hitting the high teens over the next five years – 17% in five years isn’t out of reach, but I want to see more progress before modeling that. Along similar lines, I still think long-term double-digit free cash flow margins are possible, but I’m pulling my expectations down to the high single-digits until I see more evidence of a more sustainable positive trajectory. Even with those revisions, that’s still good for long-term adjusted growth of around 10% a year.

Between discounted cash flow, margin/return-driven EV/EBITDA, ROE-driven P/BV, and other valuation approaches, I believe Nidec is around 15% to 40% undervalued now, with most of the models clustering around 25% to 40%.

The Bottom Line

I do think there has been more progress at Nidec than is reflected in the share price performance. The company is close to profitability in its traction motor business (EVs) (despite disappointing overall volumes), management has successfully pivoted toward growth opportunities like data centers and robotics, and management is not pretending that the margin situation is acceptable.

At the same time, though, margin leverage progress has been frustratingly uneven and I have to question if management is focusing on the right priorities and opportunities for the business, given that it’s willing to pursue an unfriendly buyout in a deeply cyclical industry with modest overall growth potential.

If a stock trades at a discount, there’s usually a reason and I do think the margin and strategic concerns can explain that discount. Nidec hasn’t lived up to my expectations, and it’s tempting to just walk away from the story, but I do think the valuation leaves too much upside to ignore if management can improve its execution.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Beefing My Portfolio Up With Texas Roadhouse (Earnings Review)
data.description:
Introduction

Texas Roadhouse (NASDAQ:TXRH) stock is taking a breather, but the company keeps reporting profits and pumping money toward its bottom line. While the stock is back to its mid-2024 levels, the company just reported earnings, and well... it topped estimates. In an expensive market, we have to be cautious: on one side, the risk of being caught in a bubble becomes higher, on the other, just sitting on the sidelines has a huge opportunity cost. In markets such as the one we are in, an owner mindset focused on finding great holdings at reasonable prices becomes more important every day. Otherwise, we might not be prepared to face sudden downturns, or we might not fully understand why our stocks are going up.

Now, to recap a bit of my personal investing history with Texas Roadhouse, you should know that in 2022, I came across it as I was studying a pair trade with McDonald's (MCD). At that time, I thought going long MCD and short TXRH was the right pair trade. However, as I deepened my knowledge, I saw that the pair trade was around 6 months late. Indeed, the two stocks often trade inversely when the first recession fears appear. But soon, I understood that Texas Roadhouse was a one-of-a-kind company. In the 2022 frenzy that made everyone raise prices, Texas Roadhouse willingly took a margin hit to preserve its customer loyalty and keep being perceived as offering great value. Well, this paid off, and now Texas Roadhouse is seeing the dividends of this intelligent choice. By late 2022, I capitulated and grabbed hold of some shares at a price of around $90. The stock is up 83% since then, not counting dividends. It even became a 2x before it retraced a bit. Well, after the recently released report, I am seriously considering increasing my position even though the share price is around $170. But let's look at a few numbers to understand why I say so.

Texas Roadhouse's Q4 Earnings Review

In case you are not familiar with the company, you should know that it is a restaurant company funded in 1993 and that now has over 780 restaurants in 49 states and 10 foreign countries. Its core business hinges on serving beef and steak dinners. Since its early days, the company has been able to grow and scale at a fast pace, becoming a true cash-generating machine.

In Q4, we saw a further acceleration of its results versus the rest of the year.

Total quarterly revenues increased to $1.4B, which is a 23.5% YoY growth, faster than the 16% reported for the full fiscal year. Income from operations increased 65.5% YoY to $139M (4Q23: $84M), compared to a 46% YoY growth for the year. Quarterly net income also increased by 60% to $116M (4Q23: $72M), while if we take the full-year results, we see "only" 42% YoY growth. In Q4, Texas Roadhouse reported EPS of $1.73 (+$0.09 vs. estimates) and $6.47.

Now, this is already a good report, but when we consider restaurants, we have some other metrics that help us feel the pulse of what is truly going on. Comparable sales, average weekly sales, and restaurant margin dollars are three important KPIs. Well, take a look at what Texas Roadhouse reported for the quarter:

  • comparable restaurant sales increased 7.7%
  • average weekly sales increased 8.6% to $153,867, of which $20,067 were to-go sales (+12.7% YoY). This means 13% of total weekly sales come from the to-go channel
  • restaurant margin dollars increased 37.3% to $242.6M. As a percentage of restaurant sales, they increased to 17% from 15.3%, with the main driver being higher sales. For the second consecutive year, Texas Roadhouse achieved a double-digit increase in margin dollars, which is remarkable.

What do all these numbers have in common? One sole reading: sales, sales, and sales. In other words, Texas Roadhouse's sales are trending in the right direction. With sales increasing by 7.7%, 4.9% comes from traffic, while the remaining 2.8% comes from check. Since pricing was 3.1%, there are 30 bps of negative mix mainly because of lower alcohol sales. In April, Texas Roadhouse will implement another small 1.4% price hike, and this shows, once again, how long-term-focused Texas Roadhouse is. Instead of aiming at short-lived profits, the company wanted to preserve its value proposition and has thus been able to take a temporary margin hit to make its customers happy. As inflation now comes down and consumers are less worried about their purchasing power, Texas Roadhouse can make small and regular price hikes to defend and restore its full profitability. Speaking of which, we must go over restaurant margin dollars. For those who are unfamiliar with the industry, restaurant margin dollars represent the money a restaurant earns after subtracting from its sales the cost of goods sold and other operating expenses. In other words, this is the metric that shows how efficient and profitable a restaurant can be. Increasing restaurant margin dollars at a fast pace is no easy task, but surely commodity inflation of only 0.7% in 2024 was helpful, although labor inflation of 5% was still quite impactful. All in all, what is helping restaurant margin dollars so much? The answer is quite simple: the to-go channel is becoming a golden goose for Texas Roadhouse's restaurants. After all, think about it. A restaurant can host only a certain number of guests who stay for dinner for at least one hour. Assuming they all order the most costly items, we eventually hit the maximum profit the restaurant can reach, and that can only increase through pricing. But the to-go channel requires investments only in the kitchen's productivity (capex and labor) but can then generate potentially infinite sales. These sales are higher margins because that food is sold at the same price, if not a little more, of the food consumed in a restaurant. But, the expenses faced to produce that food are lower because items such as fixed costs or waiters' wages are usually already covered by the in-restaurant sales.

Now, during the Q4 2024 earnings call, we heard that January sales are up only 5.5%, meaning there was a deceleration in growth. In addition, the first three weeks in February showed flat sales YoY. One of the reasons could be the impact of big winter storms, which make people stay at home. If this is the case, I would not be surprised to see some sort of "revenge spending": as soon as the weather stabilizes and the first signs of spring appear, people will flock back to Texas Roadhouse's restaurants as one of their first picks. Of course, during the earnings call, many analysts were a bit nervous about this slowing trend. After all, we see that restaurants are facing some challenges with traffic and sales. So far, Texas Roadhouse has distinguished itself as one of the few companies in the industry that has kept growing. However, analysts are watching out for any possible sign of deceleration or sales distress. After all, this is reflected by a 3.16% short interest (McDonald's, for example, has only 1.1%), which means some investors are betting on Texas Roadhouse's dropping in price.

Nonetheless, while the stock may even undergo some volatility, its key metrics all point in the right direction, and its growth rate keeps astonishing me. Even though it is a company whose comparable sales grow in the low teens, it trades at the same multiple of 24 that McDonald's earns. However, McDonald's growth rate has hobbled back and forth between 1.7% and 4%. On a 2026 earnings estimate, Texas Roadhouse is actually already cheaper than McDonald's. Moreover, Texas Roadhouse's stock has declined a bit in price and is now going to test the resistance around $160. This could further provide a good entry point.

Valuation

Texas Roadhouse currently earns a D- from Seeking Alpha's Factor Grade, its fwd multiples are already in C territory. Last year, the company generated around $300M in FCF. The FCF yield is around 3%, which is not extremely cheap. But considering the pace of Texas Roadhouse's growth (its FCF compounded around 13.5%) we can understand that some of this growth is being priced in. A PE around 24 right now is more or less aligned with the general market and can be considered fair for a company that has proven again and again a good stock to both protect a portfolio from a strong downside and to boost its performance with steady and growing results.

We know it might be counter-intuitive but sometimes, feeding the best picks in one's portfolio, even though they are probably appreciating assets that go up in price, can be the right thing to do. Well-managed companies with a successful business model can enjoy long periods of success. Texas Roadhouse is among them and as it approaches the critical level of $160, it might become a decent opportunity in a very expensive market.

data.title: The Mistake Of Looking For Tops In The Stock Market
data.description:
As you can imagine, dear reader, having a relatively public profile means that I receive many comments and ideas shared through social networks.

Depending on each moment or phase of the economic-stock market cycle, the type of things that reach me vary. Lately, although I have to say that by no means overwhelmingly, I receive comments such as that the US stock market is forming a top, or even that a “bull trap” seems to be brewing, where the market would form a new high and then turn violently downwards. Whichever variant is chosen, there is no doubt that we are in an era of “stock market tops” seekers.

And I am not at all surprised that this is happening, as this thought becomes quite common when the market slows down after having achieved a good rise in the previous months or quarters.

The search for market tops is most triggered in indices such as the Dow Jones Industrial Average, where the sideways process consists of a relatively steep correction, followed by a rally and where it appears visually that the final formation will be a “double top”.

However, in my experience, this is simply a psychological effect (or psychological defect) rather than a reality that is of any use in the markets. The human brain is one that is designed to detect patterns in everything it sees and hears.

I know next to nothing about evolutionary biology (or biology for that matter), but I recall that one of the characteristics that make humans intelligent beings capable of “mastering creation” is that we are able to memorize and plan, and that both tasks require our brains to be powerful pattern-processing machines.

Of course, this incredible mental capacity is one of the tools that have enabled humans to set foot on the moon, soon on Mars and who knows where in the distant future.

However, like any great power, it is often accompanied by weaknesses depending on the design of that power. For example, our brains are optimized for natural life on earth.

There are many examples, but a simple one is the one illustrated in the following image, where exactly equal circles are represented colored with a gradient from white to black. The circles that start at the top with the white gradient look like spheres to us, while the circles that start from the black color, look concave or hollow to us. So much so that, if we turn the image upside down, the spheres become hollows and the hollows become spheres. Our brain simply sees spheres or hollows depending on how the information is presented.

This psychological effect occurs because we were born on a planet with only one light source, the sun, which always illuminates from above and, therefore, through the eons, the brain of animals has been “programmed” to automatically interpret that any object with a shadow below is a convex object, while an object with a shadow above is a concave object.

But they are not, they are only drawings of circles with gradients, neither concave nor convex.

Another example of an optical illusion, which is really a mental illusion, is that of Adelson's chessboard illusion, where the color of square “A” is perceived to be darker than that of square “B”, when in fact they are the same color. If you don't believe me, download the image, go to an image editing program and capture the color code of each square. I have done it for you and the color code is #6f7070.

Well, this all happens in a similar way when we look at price charts. Our brains, optimized to survive in the wild, fall for the deception over and over again, believing that they see top patterns every time the market flattens out or goes through a sideways phase.

I am not saying that patterns cannot be observed in the market. I am saying that anything that is observed will be a serious observation when it can be accurately defined and that something that is “seen” subjectively, but without concrete measurement, falls into the high risk of being a mere optical illusion.

An investor must be very aware that the market is an artificial entity, in the sense that it has been created by human beings and, therefore, are mental projections that are not governed by the rules of nature or physics. How many times is it not said that the market gives vertigo when it has risen a lot even though the markets cannot feel that sensation and that there is no gravity in them?

All of this I can explain phenomenally well, but I can explain it not so well because of how intelligent and learned I may be, but because I have lost more than enough money, as well as the money I have missed out on, to know that this is exactly how it is.

How many tops did I fail to identify and then watch the market continue to go through the roof? Enough to know that the mind is predisposed to see those tops, when the market doesn't care what people's minds can “see”, simply because markets do not belong to the realm of the natural world.

In the very short term, of course, the market may correct, after all markets fluctuate constantly, but when it comes to investing it is better to do so based on objective elements, such as knowing whether a market is in an uptrend or not, rather than being driven by visual patterns and hunches, which on average usually yield poor results.

If the economic growth trend and the price trend are bullish, it is simply more rational to continue to maintain bullish expectations because trend following is something that can be objectively defined and because it has historically worked well on average (“momentum” investment factor).

If you have been seeing market tops in recent days and weeks, my best advice, given that economic growth persists and the price trend is up, is to follow the trend. Something so easy to say but so complicated to do.

data.title: NetEase: Undervalued Gaming Giant With Global Potential
data.description:
NetEase (NASDAQ:NTES) reported 4Q24 results with net revenue below consensus as the steady progress in the gaming business was offset by the weakness in the non-game segments. Although mobile games were soft in the Q, PC games saw stronger than expected reception.

We were also encouraged by the highly positive initial reception of Marvel Rivals and Where Winds Meet, which signaled strong global potential. The company’s new game pipeline, including Fragpunk, Destiny: Rising, and MARVEL Mystic Mayhem, presents significant opportunities to diversify and expand its market reach in 2025.

Our thesis on NTES is that the company is one of few game developers in China that has a proven track record of developing its IP, unlike Tencent, which largely mimicked proven IP, such as the case with Honor of Kings and PUBG Mobile (see: Tencent: Geopolitical Risk Is No Game), NTES has been developing proprietary IPs from its early days until now, and we believe the company has a higher chance of becoming a global game developer that can deliver AAA content to compete against those from its global peers.

With valuation trading at 9x forward EBITDA, we believe the shares are undervalued, and we prefer NTES over Tencent within the game vertical. We value NTES at 12x forward EBITDA, implying a 26% upside from the current level (See valuation below).

The gaming business remains solid

Total net revenue declined 1.4% y/y to RMB26.7bn, primarily due to underperformance in non-gaming segments, while gaming revenue was up 1.5% y/y to RMB 21.2bn, accounting for 96% of total revenue. The strength of PC games offsets the weakness of mobile games.

Despite the market challenges, we were encouraged to see a stronger sequential trend in contract liabilities in 4Q23, indicating sustainability in gaming revenue growth. However, given the higher mix of licensed games, the gross margin declined by 200ppts sequentially, but we expect the margin to stabilize as higher in-house games begin to monetize this year.

New games are off to a strong start

Marvel Rivals topped Steam’s global top sellers’ chart shortly after its launch on December 6 and established itself as a hit by accumulating 40mn players globally. The season 1 update in early January drove another strong reception on Steam. Over 10mn players joined within 72 hours, reaching 20mn across two phases post-launch. We see continuous content updates and collaborations with Marvel Comics, which are expected to sustain high player engagement.

On the other hand, Where Winds Meet is shaping up to potentially become a flagship title. The game launched with over 3mn downloads in its first four days and topped the China iOS download charts in two weeks. This title attracted over 15mn players on both PC and mobile. We are positive on Where Winds Meet as we believe this could be a global RPG hit given its unique gameplay and storyline. We believe further improvement on this series could allow NTES to deliver AAA content similar to that of Black Myth Wukong, which has taken the world by storm.

Promising gaming pipeline

Three key titles are in the pipeline that we believe will drive NTES shares higher.

Fragpunk is expected to launch on March 6. The game is a 5 v 5 tactical shooter with a mix of COD, Valorant, and CS. So far, the review has been largely positive in its beta form and this could gain some users from the other tactical shooter games such as Overwatch and Apex Legend.

In closed beta there are Destiny: Rising and MARVEL Mystic Mayhem. Destiny is another tactical shooting adventure game and is one of the most anticipated games in the market currently. As for MARVEL, the closed beta indicated good player engagement and positive feedback on the characters and gameplay, which should translate to broader adoption when it is officially released.

Valuation upside attractive with a reasonable margin of safety

NTES is currently trading at a 9x forward EV/EBITDA multiple, a significant discount relative to its global peers.

We note that Japanese game developers trade at an average 14.6x multiple, with a range between 10x and 20x, while the other Asia-based developers trade between 8x and 25x. The valuation for US peers ranges between 12.7x and 20x.

We believe that NTES should be compared against the developers with proven track records in producing IP with a long shelf life, given their track record in the FWJ series and other hits such as Where Winds Meet, which could prove to be a global success.

We believe that NTES has a higher chance of delivering AAA hits on a global level than Tencent, given NTES’s root in game development and IP creation, and we believe that 12x forward EBITDA multiple to be reasonable as it is a discount to the global Japanese peers but also on par to that of EA games given their similar growth profile

At a 12x forward EBITDA multiple, we value NTES at $26/share, or a 26% upside from the current level. Our downside scenario is that NTES trades closer to Krafton, which only has one hit title in PUBG, and this would imply an 11% downside from the current level. Given that NTES has a more robust gaming portfolio and pipeline, we do not expect the stock to trade at such low margins. Hence, we believe the downside risk is limited, while the upside risk is the successful execution of game launches that could result in shares re-rate this year.

data.title: Does Shopify Stock Deserve A Spot On Your Shopping List? (Technical Analysis)
data.description:
Thesis

After gaining over 60% in the past six months, it may be tempting to jump aboard Shopify Inc. (NYSE:SHOP) stock. In the below analysis, I find that while the near term technicals are overall bullish, there are concerning signs of bearish divergence that should concern longer horizon investors. For the fundamentals, Q4 results were very strong with revenue growth and EPS at three year highs. However, guidance was a bit weaker than desired and the stock seems moderately overvalued currently after evaluating growth projections and the sector's median P/S ratio. Therefore, I encourage investors to be cautious as I believe Shopify stock is only a hold currently due to the overall mixed signals in the technicals and the relatively unattractive fundamental setup.

Technicals

Charting

Trends

Shopify stock is in a clear uptrend since last November. The uptrend line was solid support in January and so this trend line should be expected as strong support moving forward. Currently, the stock is at a moderate distance above the trend line and so therefore, some further pullback is definitely possible.

Support

Other than the uptrend line are four other support areas in the above chart. The nearest one is at around 120 and represents major resistance back in December. The next support area is at 115 and was key resistance in November and in January while being a bit of support earlier this month. Moving down, there also seems to be support at around 107 as it represents an upside gap. Even though this gap is rather small, the volume on that day was the highest in January and so its importance is likely high. Lastly, there is distant support at 90 as there was a major upside gap at this level in November. The volume on that day was by far the highest in the past six months further adding to its significance.

Resistance

The stock is currently very near 52 week highs and therefore there is virtually no resistance for Shopify in this timeframe. There could be some resistance at the 52 week high set days ago but it is unlikely to be a major obstacle for the stock moving forward.

Takeaway

I believe it is clear that this chart is a solidly bullish one for Shopify. The stock is in a strong uptrend and there are an abundance of support levels below. As stated above, there is minimal resistance for the stock in this time frame. Overall, this chart shows that on the surface, Shopify is in a strong technical position.

Moving Averages

Simple Moving Average (SMA)

There was a golden cross in the stock back in October as the 50 day SMA broke above the 200 day SMA. The gap between the SMAs widened significantly from November to January and is holding relatively stable in February so far. This shows that long term bullish momentum is strong but is not accelerating currently.

Exponential Moving Average (EMA)

The 9 day EMA had a bullish crossover with the 21 day EMA in late January, a signal of near term strength in Shopify stock. Recently, the gap between the EMAs expanded to its greatest level yet but currently the stock just broke below the 9 day EMA. The 9 day EMA is also on a negative trajectory now, suggesting that near term bullish momentum is receding.

Bollinger Bands

The stock broke above the Bollinger Bands' midline in late January as an indication of strength in the stock. This signal should also give investors confidence that the uptrend remains strong for Shopify. Despite the recent pullback, the stock is still at a healthy level above the midline and should find support there if it corrects further.

Takeaway

Despite some minor signs of weakness in the EMAs, in my view these moving averages show a net positive technical picture for Shopify. The SMAs demonstrate stable long term bullish momentum, the EMAs are still in a bullish state overall, and the Bollinger Bands confirm that the uptrend remains strong.

Indicators

Moving Average Convergence Divergence (MACD)

The MACD had a bullish crossover with the signal line in late January. Since then, the MACD has pulled away from the signal line a bit but there has been multiple periods of contraction and expansion in the gap as shown by the mixed histogram. This shows that there has been unstable bullish control in the past few weeks. More worrying is that the MACD shows long term bearish divergence. The MACD made a much lower high at the recent 52 week high in the stock than back in early December. This potentially shows that the recent rally is on weak legs.

Relative Strength Index (RSI)

The RSI is currently at 57.47 after receding from near the 70 level recently. This indicates that the bulls remain in fair control despite losing ground to the bears. Similar to the MACD, the RSI also displays significant negative divergence. The RSI reading at the recent 52 week high is much lower that the peak in the RSI seen in November. Again, this shows that there is weakness below the surface.

Stochastics

The stochastics just had a bearish crossover as the %K broke below the %D within the overbought 80 zone. Given the sharp trajectory of the %K line it is relatively unlikely that this is a false signal even though both lines are still in the 80+ zone. Given the trajectory of the %K, the gap between these lines is also expected to expand rapidly to reflect the near term weakness in the stock.

Takeaway

In the near term, I believe these indicators show mixed signals for the stock. The MACD is still in a bullish state but there has been instability, the RSI is still above 50 but recently lost ground to the bears, and the stochastics just had a bearish crossover. For the longer term, I have significant concerns for the strength in the uptrend. The MACD and RSI both show bearish divergence and investors should be on the lookout for additional topping signals.

Fundamentals

Earnings

Quarterly Results

Shopify released their 2024 Q4 results earlier this month and showed strong results overall. They reported revenues of $2.812 billion, up 31% YoY and a diluted GAAP EPS of $0.99 versus $0.51 in the prior year period. As shown above, revenue growth is currently far above the average of the past three years and in fact it's actually at three year highs. Revenue growth has broken out of the downtrend that lasted from mid 2023 to mid 2024. For EPS, Q4 represented the strong results in the past three years as well and it is also on both a near term and long term uptrend and so Shopify seems to be in a solid position in terms of the fundamentals. Both revenue and GAAP EPS also beat expectations as they beat by $83.68 million and $0.66 respectively, showing there was very strong performance versus consensus estimates.

Guidance

Shopify provided guidance for their 2025 Q1. Firstly, they expect revenues to experience YoY growth in the mid 20s percentage range. This would be a slowdown from Q4's three year high level of growth. This Q1 growth rate would be only at slightly above average levels relative to the past few years. While they did not provide EPS guidance, they did for gross profit. They expect gross profit to have YoY growth in the low 20s percentage range. For their Q4, they had gross profit of $1.352 billion, a 27.3% increase YoY and so this guidance implies a slowdown in growth in this figure as well. Overall, I believe this guidance was a bit on the weaker side as their projections shows that a slowdown in their growth is expected for their Q1. However they did state that Q1 has been consistently their weakest Gross Merchandise Value (GMV) quarter seasonally and so some weakness here may not be overly concerning in the grand scheme of the things for Shopify.

Valuation

Price To Sales Ratio (P/S)

The P/S ratio is currently at above average levels and is rather near the three year high set back in early 2022. Currently the ratio sits at 18 after surging from around 15 since earlier this year. I believe this ratio shows moderate overvaluation in Shopify stock. As discussed above in the earnings section, revenue growth is currently at three year highs and that should justify the current high level in this ratio. However, in their Q1 guidance, they stated that they only expect growth in the mid 20s percentage range and therefore it is a moderate slowdown from Q4's growth rate. A mid 20s percentage range growth rate is only slightly above the average of the past three years and so it seems like this ratio is currently at too rich of a level to be justified by the revenue guidance. Therefore, Shopify seems moderately overvalued relative to its financial outlook.

Currently, the Information Technology sector's median P/S is 3.39 and so the stock is currently at an over 400% premium to peers. While Shopify's strong position within e-commerce helps to justify some premium to the IT sector, with revenue growth expected to slow to the mid 20s percentage range, I find the current premium to be too rich.

Overall, I conclude that Shopify is moderately overvalued at current levels with both premium to growth projections and to peers likely unjustified at this point. Investors should therefore be cautious on the stock as multiple contraction is possible.

Conclusion

In the above analysis, I have determined that the near term technicals are overall bullish for Shopify stock but the longer term divergence signals are worrying and could signal that there is weakness beneath the surface. For the fundamentals, while Q4 results were very impressive, Q1 guidance was overall weaker than desired. In addition, the valuation is currently moderately rich after accounting for the growth outlook and its relative valuation to peers in the IT sector. Since there is a mix of strong near term technicals, potentially concerning longer term divergence signals, and a relatively unattractive fundamental setup, I believe Shopify stock deserves a holding rating at this point.

data.title: AMZY Is A Role Model For The YieldMax Options Strategy
data.description:
Introduction

Regular readers of mine will know that I've not always been very kind to YieldMax funds in the past, and have given many of them sell ratings. It should be of note that I was positive about their fund-of-funds strategies like YMAG and YMAX. Overall, however, they typically don't impress me, especially the funds focusing on single stocks, like CONY, TSLY, and NVDY.

The links above all go to my most relevant articles on those funds.

Enter the latest entrant, the YieldMax AMZN Option Income Strategy ETF (NYSEARCA:AMZY), which as one can imagine, sells options against Amazon.com Inc (AMZN). Here is its price and total return since inception, back in Q3, 2023, and Amazon itself below it for reference.

One thing to note is that AMZY has experienced some price decay since its inception. This is normal for YieldMax funds, since their strategy is for income generation is so aggressive that it can cause periods of capital losses even when the underlying stock is seeing significant gains.

See the other funds I mentioned earlier for similar stories. In comparison to TSLY's 60% loss in price since AMZY's inception (TSLY is older by over a year), the fund actually doesn't look so bad.

AMZY Strategy

The basic premise for the funds is that active, daily management of options on a single-stock can produce significant income. AMZY has done this well so far, with a distribution rate almost touching 38%.

To this end, the funds are set up to create the most yield possible (hence, YieldMax). They do this in a few ways:

  • AMZY buys US Treasuries with all of its NAV
  • The fund uses those treasuries as collateral to create a synthetic long position in AMZN with options
  • AMZY sells short calls against that synthetic long position daily

In the past, I've seen YieldMax funds use traditional synthetic long positions, which consists of a long call and a short put, positioned ATM ("at-the-money," or with strike prices at or around the current market price) to create a risk/reward situation very similar to owning long stock.

This is true of AMZY as well, which has sold puts and bought calls in order to create the underlying position with the return profile of long Amazon stock, but securable with US Treasuries, and can still be used to secure short calls in the future.

AMZY Holdings

The fund is made up of three building blocks:

  1. The US Treasury Allocation, which totals 100.92% of NAV
  2. The AMZN long position, which totals -2.94% of NAV
  3. The liquid cash position, which totals 2.02% of NAV

I don't have much commentary on their treasury holdings, as they mostly consist of T-Bills and older treasuries purchased at a discount. These are standard-practice capital efficiency building blocks, and I appreciate their use here to help boost the yield another 4% without introducing significantly more risk. Instead of buying Amazon stock outright, we can get the yield of cash and still be long because the treasuries are securing the options position.

It's one of the functions of our model day trading tools, we can now build very capital efficient tools using these derivatives.

The most interesting thing, of course, is the options position.

AMZY Options Model

When we model the current options positions, we can get a P/L chart that looks like this:

For those who know Options-Speak, here are the Greeks for the total position:

Delta 1,291,584
Theta 23,578
Gamma -10,846
Vega 12,195
Rho 36,049
PoP 50%

There are two expirations with positions open on them; there are both long and short calls on the 2/21 expiration, and a traditional synthetic long position on the 4/17 expiration.

  • On the 2/21 expiration:
    • +6,630 AMZN 237.5C
    • +6,165 AMZN 240C
    • -12,795 AMZN 232.5C

That on its own looks like this, with Greeks for reference:

The negative delta tells us that this position on its own is short Amazon, which is what is generating income for the fund. As I've noted in the past, these positions tend to also carry negative Vega, which means that increased volatility may actually harm the position and cause it to lose value even if the stock trends in the right direction. That is a minor risk, however. The overall position, because of the synthetic long stock, carries a positive Vega to counteract this.

  • On the 4/17 expiration:
    • +12,795 AMZN 235C
    • -12,795 AMZN 235P

Which, on its own, looks like this, with Greeks for reference:

Like I mentioned earlier, this position has one purpose: generate Delta, or exposure to Amazon's price changes.

Note that both positions generate a credit, and are secured by the underlying treasuries. This has resulted in the fund's accumulating a nearly 105% allocation to cash and cash-equivalents.

From looking at the position as a whole and in pieces, it's clear to me that YieldMax has really figured out their methodology, albeit after a significant amount of time experimenting with these funds. The issues I raised in past articles are definitely less relevant now, and the options strategy looks cleaner than when I modeled NVDY's. The strikes chosen indicate that the managers are bullish on Amazon, and believe that they can run up, so they've given the stock some room to run before their short strikes kick in. That's very evident in the slope of the P/L chart from their total position.

This may mean that AMZY could be shorter on the stock, closer to being ATM. That would create more yield, and a strategy they've used in the past to generate up to 200% yields.

I prefer this method, and I am delighted to see that the YieldMax managers are providing some room for a bullish bias to the stock. It's unclear if this trend will actually play out, but in the past, the funds have suffered from a lack of access to the stock's upside.

This is what causes the typical underperformance of the underlying asset, being too short in order to produce income. However, with more room for the stock to move up before hitting the plateau that is the short strikes, we should expect to see less underperformance during bullish periods for the underlying stock at the expense of a higher yield.

AMZY Dividends & Yield

Generated by the short call position, AMZY sports a shiny near-38% distribution rate. This is outrageous by older standards, before options-driven funds became mainstream, and would get you laughed out of the room back in the day. Not in 2025, however, where YieldMax funds have built funds that distribute eye-watering and face-melting yields, and have returned positively for shareholders.

That is the case for AMZY shareholders, as well. While they haven't kept up with Amazon itself, they have received quite a bit of income from the options premium.

A high of $1.173/share and a low of $0.368/share shows how volatile the dividend is, with little consistency since returns are reliant on managerial skill (the fund actively trades every day), and the market moving favorably for Amazon. Since the fund also absorbs the underlying losses of AMZN.

I have been writing with the underlying assumption that investors are bullish on AMZN itself, and are willing to sacrifice upside for current income. Note that this fund is not suitable for investors bearish on Amazon itself, as the fund does incur AMZN's losses, offset by the income generated by the short positions.

Up until very recently, when Amazon experienced upside volatility that AMZY was too capped to receive, we can see how the fund very closely follows AMZN itself.

The fund has been able to capture significant amounts of AMZN's upside for most of its lifespan, likely due to the manager allowing for upside in the options strategy, and staying further OTM ("out-of-the-money," or picking strikes further away from the market price) in their short calls.

Suitability

I am issuing a hold rating for AMZY, because I don't believe that single-stock covered call funds are advisable over holding the underlying asset. There is less worry about volatility events, underperformance (which is historically true of these funds, but not all funds, looking at QDTE), or manager-risk. Assuming one is already bullish on the underlying asset, it may make more sense to sell shares in the future, or shift into a more diversified strategy like holding a fund-of-funds, which I do advise, like YMAG.

Speculative income investors are advised to limit their allocation to 1% or less to any single-stock covered call strategy like AMZY or its sister funds. In the past, I have recommended up to a 5% allocation to a fund-of-funds like YMAX.

Conclusion

Ultimately, I am impressed with the changes I've seen in YieldMax options strategy. It seems to be changing to incorporate more upside in the underlying holdings, and the YieldMax AMZN Option Income Strategy ETF (NYSEARCA:AMZY), is a perfect example of this. While I am still not a proponent of single-stock covered call funds, income investors with very high risk tolerances may be in much better hands than I have previously written about. I am changing my mind on YieldMax's managers, and their performance with funds like AMZY are reflective of their improvement in their options strategy.

Thanks for reading.

data.title: Veeco Instruments Undermined By Slowdowns In Legacy Businesses
data.description:
The outlook for Veeco Instruments (NASDAQ:VECO) looked bright in mid-2024, with the company benefitting from leading-edge demand for more advanced annealing tools, as well as growth in advanced ion beam and advanced packaging tools. Since then, though, it has become clear that Chinese producers at legacy nodes were pushing equipment orders (inflating apparent demand) and disk drive companies have slammed the brakes on capex spending, all while underlying tool demand outside of leading-edge logic and memory has been slow to rebound.

Those market dynamics have fueled meaningful negative revenue and margin revisions, and with tools companies like Veeco quite sensitive to revenue growth and margin expectations, the market reaction has been ugly. Veeco shares are down around 40% since my last update, and while several other tools companies are down double-digits as well (Applied Materials (AMAT) down about 16%, SCREEN Holdings (DINRF) down about 25%, and AIXTRON (AIXXF) down about a third), it stands to reason that the smaller, more volatile, and more China-exposed name would get hit harder.

While my growth and margin expectations are certainly lower now, the decline in the share price has outpaced those revisions. I’ll be the first to acknowledge it’s tough to have credibility on a “buy” call after such a significant disappointment, and while I thought valuation was getting somewhat stretched back in June, I think the market has overcorrected. That said, it’s likely to be a year before there’s meaningful overall revenue reacceleration and margin expansion, so investors don’t necessarily need to rush in today.

Pullbacks In Two Major Legacy Markets Are Undermining The Near-Term Outlook

The biggest changes in my outlook for Veeco relative to my last article are the prospects for the company’s legacy node business in China and its data storage tools business.

Likely looking to get ahead of potential additional restrictions on their ability to buy semi tools, Chinese fabs targeting older nodes accelerated their orders in 2024, and the outlook for 2025 is considerably weaker. Customers in China made up 36% of sales in 2024, and management is expecting that to decline to around 25% to 30% in 2025.

At the same time, the data storage business has not really recovered, with revenue in 2022, 2023, and 2024 at about 50% to 60% the level of 2021 revenue, and is going to weaken significantly in 2025 with an expected two-thirds drop in revenue at the midpoint (down to 20% of 2021 revenue). Drive manufacturers have basically stopped buying tools, and management said explicitly that “the window is closed on 2025 systems revenue”, with only spares and service revenue remaining.

The revenue hit from these legacy markets is bad enough, but both of these businesses generate above-average gross margins, and that squeezes the business further. Management has already announced that they’re looking for ways to boost the profitability of other businesses in 2025, but initial guidance is for a roughly one-point decline in gross margin from 2024 to 2025.

The timing of a recovery in either business is uncertain at this point. Demand in China has a great deal to do with U.S. trade policy going forward, and China has been working toward more independence on the semi tools side. With data storage and drive manufacturers, ongoing growth in heads per drive and the adoption of HAMR should be positive, but it’s hard to feel great about a business that has been well-off of its peak for so long now.

Leading-Edge Tool Demand Helps Soften The Blow Some

While Veeco’s legacy businesses are struggling, the company’s offerings for leading-edge logic and memory are seeing notably better conditions. To that end, management is expecting AI-driven tool revenue to double in 2025 to around 20% of sales (across advanced packaging, annealing, and ion beam tools).

Veeco continues to maintain its lead in the laser annealing tools market (a market worth around $800M today), and increased adoption of gate-all-around (or GAA) architecture is driving further LSA tool demand, as precise thermal management is even more important in GAA chip production than it was with FinFET production due to thinner, more delicate structures and more issues with self-heating. At the same time, nanosecond annealing is just starting its adoption curve, and Veeco has already shipped its first NSA tool to a logic customer (who hadn’t previously ordered an evaluation unit).

The company is also seeing solid demand in its advanced packaging tools business, including its wet processing tools that are invaluable for ensuring defect-free interconnects and various failures in 2.5D/3D assembly.

All told, management is looking for its GAA and advanced packaging businesses to double in 2025, and the company continues to be leveraged to EUV adoption (its ion beam tools are used to make mask blanks used with ASML’s (ASML) EUV tools), while also seeing some initial interest in new ion beam deposition tools for advanced DRAM production.

One ongoing wildcard worth mentioning is the company’s leverage to compound semiconductors, and particularly gallium-nitride (or GaN) and GaN-on-silicon, through its chemical vapor deposition tools.

Veeco is a relatively distant competitor in MOCVD tools to Aixtron (and to AMEC to a lesser extent), and the company has seen its market share fall from around 26% to the low double-digits over the last five years. At the same time, waiting for the growth of the GaN market has been a little like waiting for Godot and investors have become understandably frustrated with the GaN market opportunity in general.

Still, adoption of GaN is picking up, and there are legitimate applications across a number of markets or industries, including consumer power supplies (GaN is generally better for high-frequency, low/medium-voltage applications), fast charging, data center power, and high-frequency RF chips. Veeco announced that it had shipped a 300mm GaN-on-silicon tool to a “Tier 1 power device company” for evaluation, and I do still believe that GaN tools can be a source of upside for Veeco over the next five years.

The Outlook

Where it once looked like 2025 would be a year of double-digit revenue growth for Veeco, now any revenue growth at all is looking like a bullish call and margin leverage is getting pushed out further. Previously, the question was when the company could hit 20%-plus operating margins (non-GAAP) and mid-20%’s EBITDA margins, but now that’s looking at least a couple of years further off.

I do still think that Veeco can become a $1B/year revenue company, but I’m less confident that that will occur within the next five years, and my longer-term revenue growth rate has slid back toward the mid-single digits and more in keeping with its long-term performance. I do still think that low-to-mid-teens adjusted FCF margins are possible, but “possible” is far from a guarantee.

There has been a sector-wide rerating for tools companies and while a forward multiple of 22.5x once looked appropriate, I’ve cut that all the way back to 17.5x, leaving me with a fair value of around $31 on FY’26 EPS. The shares do also look undervalued on discounted cash flow and margin-driven EV/revenue, but the latter approach is particular is highly sensitive to near-term margin expectations and I don’t have as much confidence there as I’d like.

The Bottom Line

The last six to nine months have been a painful reminder that while Veeco management has done a good job of reinvesting in the business and harnessing its core IP to develop new tools for leading-edge applications, a lot of its revenue and profits are still tied to legacy businesses with much different underlying demand drivers.

I do think the correction has been overdone (as was the run-up), but the reality is that Veeco won’t see year-over-year revenue growth for a few quarters and possibly not at all in FY’25, and investors don’t like semi tools stocks when revenue is declining and margins are under pressure. That said, the market does typically anticipate the turn in these businesses by a quarter or two, so while it may be too early to get all that bullish on Veeco, this is a name worth monitoring for signs of improving orders, revenue, and margins.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Forget Rental Properties -- Why REITs Make More Sense In 2025
data.description:
Over the past few years, I have written many articles explaining why I have stopped buying rental properties and bought REITs instead.

In short, studies indicate that REITs (VNQ), on average, have earned higher returns than private real estate over the long run, and that's despite being safer and far more investments:

Study 1: REITs outperform private real estate by 4-6% per year.

Study 2: REITs outperform private equity real estate by 4% per year.

Study 3: REITs outperform private real estate by 3% per year.

Note that these studies compare averages, but many individual REITs have also done far better than that over the long run. As an example, self-storage REITs (PSA; EXR) have earned near-20% average annual total returns for the past 30 years.

But let's review this topic again and apply it to today.

Which is a better investment right now: rental properties or REITs?

I think that the question is easy to answer.

On the one hand, rentals are more expensive than ever. The average home in the United States has gained substantial value recently. This rate of annual appreciation is clearly not sustainable and has already made homeownership unaffordable to the masses:

As a result of this appreciation, the initial yields (cap rates) of rental property investments are historically low at 3-5% in most cases.

Making matters even worse, the cost of debt has surged significantly.

Today, the average interest rate on a 30-year mortgage is over 7%. This makes it impossible to self-finance properties today with the use of a mortgage. The only exception is if you could get a cap rate exceeding 7%, but that would be truly exceptional in today's market. You would likely need to buy a risky asset with a lot of hair in a bad neighborhood to make it work somehow.

So, to recap, rental properties are expensive today, offer low initial yields, and you can't finance them in a way that is cash flow positive.

Therefore, many rental property investors have turned to buying properties all cash, hoping to add a mortgage on them someday later when mortgage rates return to lower levels to pull their equity out. This allows them to get into the market, hoping to make up for the low yield with some appreciation down the line.

But how likely is it that homes appreciate further in this market?

In the long term, they likely will, but right now, housing is already the least affordable it has ever been, and transaction activity has dropped significantly.

So, I would not expect any significant appreciation in the upcoming years. On the contrary, it wouldn't surprise me at all if prices drop a bit, especially if interest rates remain at these high levels.

Now, let's compare that to REITs

Despite real estate prices increasing significantly over the past years, REITs are down about 25% on average:

REITs sold off as interest rates surged, and investors pulled their capital out of REITs to invest in bonds instead.

That's one downside of being a liquid asset. It facilitates such moves, and it is clear that many investors weren't interested in owning REITs for the long run. They owned them as bond alternatives, but then sold them at the first chance of getting a decent yield from fixed income.

As a result of this, REITs are priced at decade-low valuations today, with many REITs trading at 30, 40, or even 50% discounts relative to the value of the assets they own.

This lower valuation explains why private equity real estate giants like Blackstone (BX) and Starwood have been buying a lot of REITs lately.

Real estate is cheaper in the public market than in the private market right now. It makes no sense to pay a premium to own a private concentrated work-intensive illiquid asset when you could get a better deal for a public liquid diversified passive investment.

"By the way, when credit comes back, you are gonna see REITs take off... There are some unbelievable bargains in REITs. We did the same thing during the pandemic. We bought a dozen stocks all over the world and we had a 70% IRR on that stuff. We are already buying some stuff in the public market..." Barry Sternlicht, CEO/Chairman, Starwood.

But REITs also offer another major benefit.

Not only are they cheaper, but they also provide you with access to debt at a much lower cost.

When you buy shares of a REIT, you provide the equity, and the REIT then adds debt on top of it. This means that your equity enjoys the benefits of leverage in the same way as if you were buying a rental property.

The difference is that most REITs took mortgages years ago when interest rates were much lower and locked in their rates for a long time to come. To give you an example, NNN REIT (NNN) has an average remaining debt term of 12 years, and its average interest rate is just 4%.

By investing in the equity of NNN, you also get the benefits of its lower interest rates.

It is the same as if you were buying a property and assuming its debt at the same time. If that debt has a low interest rate and attractive terms, it would typically increase the value of the property, but in the case of REITs, the market is not giving them any premium; on the contrary, it is pricing them discounted, as we explained earlier.

So, to recap, REITs allow you to buy real estate discounted and with the added benefit of lower interest rates.

With that in mind, why would you buy a rental property instead?

It just doesn't make sense to me.

If you are still not convinced, take a look at the next chart.

It shows that REITs have historically been especially rewarding investments after periods like today when they were heavily discounted relative to the value of the real estate they own.

They have nearly doubled investor's money in the next three years.

I think that many REITs offer similar return prospects going forward as the REIT market eventually recovers from its 3-year-long bear market.

What's the catalyst?

Rent growth is expected to accelerate in 2026 as new supply drops significantly right as interest rates are cut further. The combination of these two things should significantly boost the market sentiment of the REIT sector, and we predict that it will lead to substantial upside across the board.

Some REITs will, of course, rise more than others, and that is why we are very selective at High Yield Landlord.

data.title: Alstom: Lower Upside Remains, But Still A 'BUY' Here
data.description:
Dear readers/followers,

One of the companies I have been covering as of late, the last 1-3 years, is the French company Alstom (OTCPK:ALSMY). I invested both before the significant drop when there was a real danger the company might have to tap equity or get into such things, and also after. The second part of that investment has seen absolutely massive returns given the surge of recovery the company has seen since.

For this article, we have two factors that, in my view, warrant an update. First, the company's third quarter of 24/25 was reported during the end of January 2025. This news will serve to sharpen the thesis and long-term/short-term expectations. Secondly, the company has some investor updates not related to the 3Q24 as well, with new cooperations with Deutsche Bahn and others. Using these pieces of news, I will update my overall thesis on this company and see where the upside comes in.

It should be noted that I am now at a good, solid RoR for the company, as opposed to in the negative as I was before. Still, when we're talking about a company like this where I have a very high conviction, I have not yet experienced a situation where I haven't gotten a good rate of return out of the investment - eventually. Though some of those investments are still ongoing, and it's been years. But this is how I invest - very long term.

For this article, the native ticker is ALO, and the relevant ADR is ALSMY.

Let's look at what we have here.

Alstom - Upside from an infrastructure company with significant long-term potential.

Alstom is up there with companies like Siemens (OTCPK:SIEGY), which unfortunately for Alstom has a much better risk/reward profile and diversification. Siemens is "ahead" of Alstom. Up till a year ago, Alstom was still digesting some of the Bombardier poor profit orders that they got as part of the deal with the Canadian business.

Now, however, things are looking much better. This is also reflected by the company's share price. I'm sorry to say that I think this company has moved up "too fast". I would have loved to have more time to really establish a massive position at a very cheap valuation like we had in January of last year. There was no doubt in my mind that Alstom was not going to go bankrupt, but unfortunately, I allowed myself to be detracted by dividends and other things, which led to me missing out quite a bit on this.

Oh, I bought it. But one of the things I have noted is that I'm too "chicken" at times in pulling the big triggers in situations like this. Go in with a full 1-2% when the upside is as clear as it, to me, was at the time.

So what I have now is a good return. A decent one. Could have been better.

For the 3Q period, Alstom reported very solid results in line with what you see above in terms of expectations and forecasts. 9M order intake is above €15.2B, with sales up 5.3% for the year, and a significant, over 5% organic increase. The company's fiscal-year outlook was improved to a book-to-bill above 1x, and organic growth above 5%, which is above my forecast range. Also, FCF is at €300-€500M, which should be the first step in the company starting to discuss the reintroduction of its dividend.

Some might call that too soon. They might say that the company is still in the process of rebalancing its overall mix, and is currently seeing a positive trend from a supportive market for rail traffic and that this can go down just as fast. And there is some of that. If you look at the short-term trends above, there has been a bit of normalization since my last article, where I also urged some "caution" despite my "Buy", it's right there in the title of that latest article, which you can find here.

It goes to show you that the stock market trends are "alive", and there is a tendency to overreact, in both ways.

However, when it comes to Alstom, as you see above, I say that the company is one of the safer infrastructure businesses in all of Europe. The biggest risk to the intra-year stability of this company is probably the French shareholding in the structure - but with the introduction of Bombardier, now over 50% of the company's shareholders geographically are actually based in North America, which is a big change. The company is therefore now a company with a large institutional, but also large international shareholding base.

And even if you look at the company from a long-term potential, you can see that the slump the company currently could be argued to be in is likely to be very short-term. Alstom's target is to return FCF to 100% over the cycle, and the FCF forecast means that I don't think dividends are far off, and the dividend level I expect is perhaps around the €0.25/share level - not that much, we're talking a yield at €20/share of about 1.25% - but still a nice addition to an otherwise-also great upside that we have to look at here.

The addition of Bombardier transportation is transformative to the business overall. The company is working with E-locomotives, with Monorails and with people movers through its brands TRAXX, Innovia, and others, which come at a high degree of complementary advantages for the already existing rolling stock portfolio. There are already significant amounts of investments confirmed in the segment going forward, and there is a high degree of geographical complementarity considered here as well.

Value creation for Alstom will come from a few places. I believe the company's rolling stock and components will serve as the major growth market here, given the company's exposure to this. But the addition of Bombardier also means a great/large service addition, with profitability that is better (at this time) than the rolling stock segment, which unfortunately is still seeing breakeven results, though expecting improvement. Digital & Integrated systems is also improving, with margin improvements. Eventually, I believe the three segments will all be working in concert, with Rolling stock below double digits (but high single digits), and the two smaller segments where the company earns more of its profits.

To summarize, as of early 2025, the company offers a very comprehensive portfolio of complementary and attractive infrastructure and infrastructure-related services. This sets it apart from most competitors, which either do one or the other. Europe is the core segment for the company, at over 60% of the company's top line, and I forecast continued growth in this market above 4.5% CAGR, because of heavy infrastructure investments across the continent.

This unfortunately does not mean that Alstom is the best pick here. The company is now above €20/share, and this is close to the price that I had overall when I began investing not that long ago in the company.

Risks to Alstom at this time include China's pursuit of non-Chinese growth, but I do think that they face investment hurdles when marketing the CRCC assets - though provided they can show quality, this might change. Also, if some of the smaller peers in service or signaling merged, they could offer real competition to the company in key segments, which I am sure they are aware of.

And last, as I have said before, I believe the market is perhaps taking the turnaround of Bombardier a bit too early here, as there are still contracts and unprofitabilities to work through.

Despite very solid January 2025 results, the valuation for the company remains as follows in terms of the upside.

Valuation - Cutting my price target due to more Bombardier challenges, and potentially lower and later dividends

As of this article, I'm slightly cutting my price target for Alstom down to €29/share. I do this for two reasons. First off, the company's near-term/short-term upside is likely to be lower than expected, closer to €1.4/share for the year, and perhaps more challenging in terms of growth going forward. Also, the company's plan to delever the company will come at a higher cost (I believe) than I originally estimated, with potential bigger headwinds. My new PT is €3/share less than before, down to €29/share. I continue to be more positive than the overall market.

The upside including increased availability of capital for maintaining and building new infrastructure is a general theme across the world, and this directly impacts Alstom positively. It means, as I believe, higher relative growth in service revenue, which in turn supports margin expansion while reducing the cyclicality of the company's top line.

From the historical and forward-projected perspective, I say that Alstom is likely to grow above 15% even beyond the 2025E fiscal period every year, and even just using a 12-13x P/E, this implies around a €29-€30/share price at this time (Source: F.A.S.T graphs Paywalled Link).

Even in say, the case of a 10x P/E for the company using these forecasts, that is still a return above 10% per year, and that's the lowest I would be willing to forecast here.

From a peer perspective, which is the secondary approach I use in establishing my long-term valuation targets, Alstom would be comparable to the aforementioned Siemens AG, which has a higher valuation/overvaluation, but also companies like Bombardier, which is certainly cheap here. Of course, there's a reason Bombardier is cheap, and I wouldn't necessarily invest in it at this time. The current peers are currently either higher-valued, or not as attractive as Alstom is, and for that reason, I continue to consider Alstom the highest among this type of infrastructure investment.

The ADR here is ALSMY, and my updated price target of €29/share translates to, based on the 0.1x ADR to around $3/share.

While moving above the native €20/share has certainly taken some of the "shine" off this company, the upside remains, and I give the company the following thesis here.

Thesis

  • Based on long-term positive business fundamentals from a combined rolling stock/service/turnkey business and added to by urbanization in the emerging markets, the future trends for Alstom are likely to be positive. Pushes for green services and marketing are likely to enhance this even further. My positive thesis has already to at least 50-60% materialized, but this leaves less near-term upside to be gained here, especially until the dividend is reintroduced.
  • The company's focus on improving portfolio margins and moving to higher-margin segments such as signals/services is likely to benefit the company in the long term. This remains the case as of the latest quarter as well.
  • I still view the company as a "Buy", but I am lowering my PT down to €29/share to account for a longer and somewhat more difficult "trip" up. I believe you can invest here, I say it's a "BUY", but it would now pay off to be a bit more careful with the company. It is no longer "cheap".

My short-term investment case (6-12 months) is as follows: Based on continued Bombardier profitability adjustments and the last of the contract executions, I see the potential for near-term volatility in the company's share price. You have already seen how the company goes up and down, with the latest move down from almost €24 to back below €20/share. More is possible short term - but I do think the catalyst for a significant downturn short term isn't there without an external factor.

My medium-to-long-term investment case (up to and beyond 5 years) is as follows: For the longer-term 2-5 year thesis, I see the possibility of the company moving first toward the €25/share and then towards the €30/share mark as quite likely given the fundamentals of which I have spoken. As a company focusing on infrastructure, I believe the large-scale infrastructure markets are very supportive here, which translates to a positive view of Alstom.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (italicized):

  • This company is overall qualitative.
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative & well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside based on earnings growth or multiple expansion/reversion.

The company fulfills 3 out of 3 quality indicators, and 1 out of 2 valuation indicators. That means 4/5 "stars" here, which is enough for an attractive thesis for the company. Based on this, and a valuation target of €29/share where I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that's attractive enough for me. I give the company a "Buy" rating as of February 2025. I will, however, no longer call the company "cheap" here.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Cenovus: Bargain With An Excellent Shareholder Yield
data.description:
Article Thesis

Cenovus Energy Inc. (NYSE:CVE) reported its Q4 results on Thursday, with the report showing relatively weak profitability due to bad results from CVE's refining business. Still, cash generation in 2024 remained strong, and I believe that cash generation will be healthy in the current year as well. With an undemanding valuation, some production growth, and compelling expected shareholder returns, Cenovus Energy could be a good investment for 2025 and beyond.

Past Coverage

I have written about Cenovus Energy Inc. here on Seeking Alpha in the past. My most recent article on the company is from early December 2024, close to three months ago. I was bullish on the company back then and remain so despite the rather weak profitability shown for the fourth quarter. Shares got even cheaper since my most recent article on CVE, due, in part, to worries about tariffs against Canada -- I believe these worries are overblown. With these macro fears making Cenovus even cheaper than before, and with the company having reported its most recent earnings results this week, it is time for an update on this Canadian oil player.

Cenovus: Macro Fears Make For A Buying Opportunity

Shares of Cenovus Energy are down over the last month and on a year-to-date basis, despite the fact that the broad market has rallied to new all-time highs. This relatively weak share price performance in 2025 has been driven by investors' worries about what the US administration could do to Cenovus Energy and its Canadian energy peers.
